content_en:
The second chapter addresses tahara (ritual purity), which Islamic scholars traditionally place at the beginning of every fiqh compendium because purity is a precondition for the most fundamental acts of worship. Sheikh al-Fawzan opens with the hadith of the Prophet, peace be upon him, that 'purification is half of faith' (al-tuhur shatar al-iman), establishing purity as a matter of deep spiritual significance, not merely a procedural formality.

The chapter systematically covers the types of water permissible for purification: mutlaq (absolute) water that may be used for all types of purification, used water whose ruling scholars have discussed, and water mixed with pure or impure substances. Sheikh al-Fawzan explains the Hanbali distinction between water that has changed in color, taste, or smell due to an impurity versus water whose qualities have changed due to contact with permissible substances.

The author then covers the categories of ritual impurity. Minor impurity (hadath asghar) is removed through wudu (ablution), while major impurity (hadath akbar) — caused by sexual intercourse, seminal emission, menstruation, and post-natal bleeding — requires ghusl (full body washing). The chapter provides detailed guidance on the obligatory elements (fara'id) and recommended acts (sunan) of both wudu and ghusl, drawing on the relevant hadith evidence for each element.

Tayammum (dry ablution using clean earth) is covered as the substitute for water when water is unavailable or when using water would cause harm. Sheikh al-Fawzan explains the conditions that permit tayammum, its procedure, and the rulings on when it is invalidated. The chapter concludes with a discussion of najasah (physical impurities) — their categories, what they are, and how garments, bodies, and surfaces are purified from them — providing the practical foundation for valid prayer and other acts of worship. Sheikh al-Fawzan's treatment of tahara reflects the Hanbali school's approach of grounding every ruling firmly in Quranic verse and authentic hadith, with clear indication of where scholarly consensus exists and where legitimate differences of opinion among the schools are acknowledged. This commitment to evidential transparency allows readers not only to learn what the rules are but to understand why they are as they are, fostering the deeper comprehension that enables confident practice.
content_id:
Bab kedua membahas thaharah (bersuci secara ritual), yang secara tradisional ditempatkan oleh para ulama fikih di awal setiap kompendium fikih karena kesucian adalah prasyarat bagi ibadah yang paling mendasar. Tanpa bersuci, shalat tidak sah — dan shalat adalah tiang agama.

Syekh al-Fawzan membahas thaharah secara sistematis: jenis-jenis air (air mutlak, air musyammas, air musta'mal, dan air yang tercampur najis), wudhu beserta syarat, rukun, sunnah, dan hal-hal yang membatalkannya, mandi wajib dan sebab-sebabnya, tayammum sebagai pengganti ketika tidak ada air atau ada uzur, hukum-hukum najis dan cara menyucikannya, serta masalah-masalah istinja' dan istijmar. Setiap pembahasan disertai dalil dari Al-Quran dan Sunnah serta pendapat mazhab Hanbali yang dikuatkan dengan dalil.
